Everyone copies his buy. Almost nobody survives his wait.

Raamdeo AgrawalThe house philosophy he built it on: Buy Right, Sit Tight.

05
What most people miss.
The return came from the dead years, not the buy.
Three flat years and a broken JV scared everyone out — right before the money was made.
06
One question
If your best stock went nowhere for three years, would you still be holding it?
Be honest. That stretch is where the return actually lives.
Save this for the next time your best idea goes quiet.

Education only. Not investment advice. Figures rounded and illustrative. Sources: AlphaIdeas; Motilal Oswal 30th Wealth Creation Study; Wikipedia; Value Research (quote); Forbes (net worth, Apr 2024).

Caption — Instagram post body

Everyone copies the buy. Almost nobody copies the wait.

In 1995, Raamdeo Agrawal put about ₹10 lakh into Hero Honda at roughly ₹30 a share. He sold around 2015 near ₹2,600. Roughly 85x on price alone, plus dividends that were throwing off ₹3–4 crore a year by the end. The internet loves that part. It's a great screenshot.

Here's the part the highlight reel skips. From March 2000 to March 2003 — the whole dot-com mania — that stock sat near ₹225 and did nothing. Three dead years. Then in 2011 the Honda joint venture broke up and the Street wrote the company off. He didn't sell. He judged the moat — the brand, the Munjals, the market lead — was still intact. That's the "L" in his QGLP framework: longevity of the competitive advantage. He built moat thinking into Indian equity research through the Wealth Creation Study, now in its 30th edition, long before "moat" was a word retail used here.

The lesson isn't "find a compounder." Everybody can find a compounder. The lesson is the dead years are not a bug — they're the entry fee for the return. Most people quietly bail during the nothing-happening stretch, which is the exact stretch that pays.

One honest caveat: he runs a brokerage and an AMC that earn more when clients stay invested. So "sit tight" is real conviction and good for business at the same time. Both can be true. Hold the idea, keep the skepticism.

He didn't out-pick the market. He out-sat it. Same money, same stock — the difference was the years he refused to flinch. Now you've got AI on your side to flag the panic before you act on it.
